issue_title: Growth, Water Scarcity, and Long-Term Supply in Nevada
input: working/nevada/water-scarcity/evidence-pack.json (108 policy bills of 189 collected; 2019-2025; recall audit 2026-07-17 added Tahoe/rivers/flood/sewage headings and checked all special sessions)

2019.note: highest pass rate in the record

2023.note: first vetoes appear in the record

2025.note: worst pass rate in the record; most failures stopped at the first committee

theme_scorecards.label: Money for water projects
theme_scorecards.typical_stop: the three failures died early: two in a money committee (82:SB102, 83:SB159), one in first committee (81:SB73 Reno flood funding)
theme_scorecards.inference: Inference: appropriations and grant-program bills nearly always finish once they reach a floor; the filter is the money committee, not the vote.

theme_scorecards.label: Water permits and the State Engineer's rules
theme_scorecards.typical_stop: bigger changes stop in first committee or first floor (80:AB30 was the exception: passed Assembly 31-9, died in Senate)
theme_scorecards.inference: Inference: narrow, technical permit fixes often moved; bills adding new duties or conditions on the State Engineer moved less.

theme_scorecards.label: Water utilities, districts, and local agencies
Clean water and pollution
theme_scorecards.typical_stop: scattered; the constitutional clean-water right (82:AJR3) died in first committee; Tahoe cleanup-program bonds passed every session

theme_scorecards.label: Using less water
theme_scorecards.typical_stop: 2025 SB143 (water-saving review + turf study) passed the Senate 21-0 and died in the Assembly
theme_scorecards.inference: Inference: conservation mandates on suppliers often moved; the record's most famous water law (81:AB356 grass ban) is here.

theme_scorecards.label: Colorado River, planning, studies, and new supply
theme_scorecards.typical_stop: asking-the-feds resolutions pass; restructuring agencies dies in first committee (80:SB76, 81:AB15); planning mandates got vetoed in 2023 (82:SB169)
theme_scorecards.inference: Inference: cloud seeding is the only new-supply idea that received state money in this record - twice, unanimously.

theme_scorecards.label: Groundwater, wells, and over-pumped basins
theme_scorecards.typical_stop: first committee (5 of 8 non-enactments); 82:SB180 passed both houses 41-0 and unanimous, still no law; the 2025 retry (83:AB363) died on the Assembly floor calendar

theme_scorecards.label: Buying, banking, or retiring water rights
theme_scorecards.typical_stop: failed in 2021 (81:AB354 water banking, first committee) and 2023 (82:SB176, first floor), then 83:SB36 became law in 2025
theme_scorecards.inference: Inference: this idea took three sessions to land; recently done - groups may ask whether SB36 covers what they want or leaves gaps (it is limited to over-appropriated basins and depends on funding).

theme_scorecards.label: Courts and oversight of water decisions
theme_scorecards.typical_stop: first committee (3); two got one-chamber floor wins in 2023-2025 and died in the second house (82:AB387 26-14; 83:SB31 15-6)
theme_scorecards.inference: Inference: 0-for-6 across four sessions, but the last two attempts got further than the first four - movement without a finish.

theme_scorecards.label: Data centers, big water users, and tax deals
theme_scorecards.typical_stop: bills that would restrain data centers or big tax breaks died early or at the wire; bills that helped them passed
theme_scorecards.inference: Inference: direction matters in this theme. Helping large users: 4 for 4 (80:SB547, 80:AB400, 81:AB66, 82:AB261). Restraining them: 0 for 5 (83:AB77, 82:SB394, 82:SB429, 81:AB449, and 83:SB364 which stalled). The one water-cooling bill (83:AB385) tried to protect evaporative cooling from local bans and died in its first committee.

proposal_reality_cards.citizen_proposal: Develop new water supply through desalination - partnering with Arizona/Mexico on a Sea of Cortez pipeline, or funding California desalination in exchange for Colorado River water.
proposal_reality_cards.what_lawmakers_tried: Nothing in this record. Zero bills in 2019-2025 proposed, funded, or studied desalination or an out-of-state supply exchange.

closest_bills.note: 2019 study of water treatment and recycling - passed first committee, died on the Assembly floor
2023 cloud seeding money - became law (the only 'make more water' idea funded in this record)
2025 cloud seeding money again - became law

proposal_reality_cards.venue_note: Colorado River trades run through the seven-state compact system and federal law; an international pipeline would need Congress and Mexico. The state tool used so far for interstate asks is a resolution (82:SJR3 passed 42-0, urging federal action). Money and studies, though, are fully within state control - and none have been proposed for desalination.
proposal_reality_cards.chokepoint: No chokepoint yet - the idea has never been filed.
proposal_reality_cards.who_carried_adjacent: Committee-sponsored resolutions; Desert Research Institute appropriations carried through money committees.
open_questions: Would a study or feasibility appropriation - the usual first step in this record - find a sponsor?
Which parts need only Nevada (money, studies) and which need other states or Congress?
Cloud seeding won funding twice with no opposition. What made that new-supply idea fundable when nothing else was tried?

proposal_reality_cards.citizen_proposal: Build an interstate water pipeline / declare a national water emergency - a federal project modeled on Hoover Dam, the TVA, and the Interstate Highway System.
proposal_reality_cards.what_lawmakers_tried: No bill. The legislature's only moves toward Washington were resolutions: 82:SJR3 (2023) urging the Bureau of Reclamation on Colorado River management passed both houses 42-0 and 21-0; 81:AJR2 and 81:AJR3 (2021) on watershed protection passed at least one house.

closest_bills.note: urged federal Colorado River action - passed unanimously
forest-and-water resolution - passed, needs second passage
restructuring the Colorado River Commission - died in first committee (2021 AB15 retried; same result)

proposal_reality_cards.venue_note: A federal mega-project is decided in Congress, not Carson City. What the state controls: resolutions (cheap to pass in this record), the Colorado River Commission's shape and instructions (attempts died twice), and money for studies or lobbying (never proposed for this).
proposal_reality_cards.chokepoint: Not applicable at the state level; agency-restructuring attempts stopped in Senate Government Affairs / Assembly first committees.
proposal_reality_cards.who_carried_adjacent: Resolutions were committee-sponsored; CRC restructuring came from Sen. Pete Goicoechea (R) and Assembly Natural Resources.
open_questions: Is a unanimous resolution a real lever or a low-cost gesture? What followed SJR3?
Who in Nevada's congressional delegation would need to carry a federal project, and what has the legislature formally asked of them?
If the state's own river agency couldn't be restructured twice, what does that say about appetite for bigger interstate plays?

proposal_reality_cards.citizen_proposal: Regulate data centers: require closed-loop cooling and self-sufficient power, no open evaporative cooling - broad agreement that data centers should 'pay their own way.'
proposal_reality_cards.what_lawmakers_tried: Eleven bills touch data centers or the tax deals that recruit them. None regulates their water or cooling. The scoreboard by direction: bills easing rules or keeping incentives for large users went 4 for 4 (80:SB547 utility exemption for data-center power gear; 80:AB400 kept school-tax abatements workable; 81:AB66 abatement housekeeping; 82:AB261 added water-efficiency language to the state economic development plan). Bills restraining large users or their tax breaks went 0 for 5: 83:AB77 (let the state deny abatements, data centers named - died in Assembly Revenue, its first committee), 82:SB394 (cap abatements at $500,000 - passed the Senate 14-7, died in Assembly Revenue at the deadline), 82:SB429 (tie abatements to paid family leave - vetoed), 81:AB449 (tighten abatement agreements - first committee), 83:SB364 (expand abatements - also stalled). The one cooling bill, 83:AB385, pushed the other way: it would have banned local governments from prohibiting big water-based cooling towers, and died in its first committee.
proposal_reality_cards.venue_note: Nevada has no state law on data-center water or cooling. The Southern Nevada evaporative-cooling ban (2023-2024) is a water-authority/local development rule, not a statute - which is why 83:AB385 attacked it through building-code preemption. Water dedication rules for new large users differ by region (Truckee Meadows requires dedicated rights plus an 11% drought reserve; southern Nevada works differently under Lake Mead rules). Interim committees spent 2026 studying data-center water and energy for the 2027 session.
proposal_reality_cards.chokepoint: Assembly Revenue killed both abatement-restraint bills (83:AB77, 82:SB394 after Senate passage). AB385's preemption died in Assembly Government Affairs. That committee failure cut both ways in 2025: it also stopped the industry-friendly cooling bill.
proposal_reality_cards.who_carried_adjacent: Restraint side: committee-sponsored (Assembly Revenue for AB77; Senate Revenue for SB394). Preemption side: Asm. Lisa Cole (R) for AB385. The 2019 enabling bill SB547 was carried by Sen. Chris Brooks (D) with broad Democratic co-sponsorship and passed near-unanimously.
open_questions: The 2027 session follows an interim study of exactly this topic. Which committee will own the bill - Natural Resources (water) or Revenue (tax deals)?
The abatement-cap bill passed the Senate 14-7 with committee sponsorship in 2023. What blocked it in the Assembly, and is that blocker still there?

Closed-loop requirements exist nowhere in statute. Would a rule live in building codes (local), water law (State Engineer), or abatement conditions (GOED)? Each path stopped in a different committee in this record.
Industry says new builds are closed-loop already. What reporting would let anyone verify that - and has any bill proposed usage reporting? (None in this record.)

people_and_process_signals.committee_vs_person_sponsored: 58 of 108 policy bills came from committees; 50 from named lawmakers.

frequent_primaries.name: Sen. Pete Goicoechea
frequent_primaries.note: groundwater, districts, retirement of rights; some enacted, several stalled

frequent_primaries.name: Asm. Howard Watts
frequent_primaries.note: conservation and clean-water bills across all four sessions

people_and_process_signals.committee_chokepoints: Of 27 policy bills that died in their first committee, 15 died in a Natural Resources committee. Assembly Revenue stopped both data-center abatement-restraint bills. Money committees (Finance/Ways & Means) stopped 2 appropriations.
people_and_process_signals.veto_watch: 2023 produced 3 vetoes in this set (82:AB97, 82:SB169, 82:SB429) - the governor became a live stop for planning mandates and abatement conditions.

high_support_non_enactments.note: groundwater boards; died at session end
resolution; passed but is only a request
abatement cap; died in Assembly Revenue
best available science; died in Senate

recent_enactments_watchlist.note: state account to buy and retire water rights in over-pumped basins - recently done; groups may ask whether funding and scope match the need
public notice for pollution permits (cross-party sponsors)
simpler rate changes for small water utilities (with 83:SB326)

deliberation_prompts: No desalination or pipeline bill has ever been filed here. Is that because the ideas are unrealistic, or because no one has asked a sponsor yet? What would a first step (a funded study) cost?
Cloud seeding got state money twice with zero no votes. Why did that new-supply idea move when nothing else was tried?

Data-center tax breaks survived every attempt to cap or condition them, while the state's economic development plan now must consider water efficiency. Is the lever the tax deal, the water law, or the local permit?
The abatement cap (82:SB394) passed the Senate and died in Assembly Revenue at a deadline. Is that a 'no' to the idea or a calendar death?
82:SB180 passed with zero no votes and still didn't become law; its 2025 retry (83:AB363) also died late. What has to change for a third try to finish?
Oversight-of-the-State-Engineer bills are 0 for 6, but the last two won a floor vote in one house. Is support growing, or is the second house the permanent wall?
Three 2023 bills died by veto, not by vote. How should a group weigh the governor when judging what is realistic?
15 of 27 first-committee deaths happened in Natural Resources committees. Who sits there now, and what have they advanced before?
data_limits_carried_forward: Discovery is keyword search plus the LCB subject index - broad, not provably complete.
The record shows where bills stopped, never why.
Committee Yea votes are inferred from membership minus recorded Nay/Absent (marked in source).
Party labels from official NELIS rosters; 100% of ballot rows matched.
The 2026 interim data-center study is context from official documents, not part of the bill record.
